text: This article tries to understand the concept of illiberalism with the aim of rendering it more operational for empirical research. The term has been used in an inconsistent way, often simply identifying it with forms of modern democratic regression or with right-wing politics. Focusing on the underlying liberal principles of limited state power, state neutrality and an open society, this paper defines illiberalism as the ideological rejection of these principles, thereby turning it into a more generic concept applicable to a broader set of contexts and ideas. This makes illiberalism into a diversum — syndrome of ideas — rather than an ideology. The second part of the paper tries to provide more details regarding the concepts surrounding illiberalism, i.e. populism and authoritarianism and presents a broader typology, where nine possible routes to illiberalism are identified: populist, authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, paternalist, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, materialist-technocratic and left-wing.
